psychology.title: Neurological Escapism
psychology.subtitle: The Cognitive Flight Mechanism
psychology.content: Escapism isn't just daydreaming; it's a 'dissociative state' triggered by the Prefrontal Cortex to mitigate chronic stress. While restorative escapism (low-dose) builds resilience, avoidant escapism (high-dose)—like Charley's obsession—leads to 'Maladaptive Daydreaming.' This is a neurological attempt to replace cortisol-inducing reality with dopamine-rich nostalgia.

comparison.title: The Stress Dialectics
comparison.subtitle: 1894 Industrialism vs. 2026 Digitalism
comparison.left_text: Characterized by 'Neurasthenia' or nerve-shattering. The stress was physical and structural—rapid urbanization, the noise of steam, and the rigid social hierarchies of the post-Civil War era.
comparison.right_text: Characterized by 'Cognitive Overload.' The stress is invisible and omnipresent—algorithmic anxiety, the death of privacy, and the 'Infinite Scroll' which acts as a modern-day Third Level.

history.title: Architectural Liminality
history.subtitle: Grand Central as a Temporal Portal
history.content: Grand Central Terminal is a 'liminal space'—a place of transition where time feels suspended. Built on a celestial map (the zodiac ceiling), it represents the human desire to connect the mundane (trains) with the eternal (stars). In Finney's narrative, the station is a biological entity, a 'tree' growing roots into the past to save those drowning in the present.

verdict.title: The Final Verdict
verdict.subtitle: Solution or Fatal Illusion?
verdict.content: The Third Level is a fatal siren song. To accept the 1890s as a 'solution' is to ignore the progress of medicine, rights, and truth. Charley doesn't find a solution; he finds a hiding spot. True psychological health requires 'Active Adaptation'—changing the present—rather than 'Passive Regression' into a fictionalized past.
